coredata.dc:title: Waste-derived high-yield biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots for biological and nanofertiliser applications
coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Monikankana
Hazarika, Abhishek
Roy, Kallol
Khare, Puja
Dihingia, Anjum
Konwar, Rituraj
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: Petroleum coke and kitchen tea residues are solid waste containing significant carbon contents. In this novel work, we have efficiently fabricated high quantum yield fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from petroleum coke (Pet coke/PC) and kitchen tea residues (KTR) by using a simple and environmentally friendly process of chemical oxidation induced by ultrasonic energy. The average particle dimension of the fabricated PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs are 6 nm and 4.5 nm, respectively revealing highly water-soluble possessing excitation-dependent fluorescence with a maximum photoluminescent quantum yield of 55.14 %. The cell-biology studies demonstrated their excellent biocompatibility with anti-cytotoxic, no significant reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, and are non-genotoxic behaviors. Certain free radical scavenging activity was also observed, representing the presence of sensible antioxidant properties in the waste-derived CQDs. In addition, KTR-CQDs possesses excellent antibacterial properties against different bacterial strains. Further, the phytological investigation with Stevia rebaudiana plants demonstrates the promising potential of waste-derived CQDs to serve as a nanofertilizer, thereby enhancing plant growth. This study opens up a new avenue for gainful and sustainable utilization of industrial waste.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PC-CQDs at 20 nm, (b) corresponding HRTEM image at 2 nm displaying the 0.26-nm lattice spacing (SAED patterns in inset indicates the presence of (002), (100), (001), and (004) planes of graphitized carbon), (c) particle size distribution representing 3–10-nm sizes of PC-CQDs. (d) EDS assessment represents the existence of C,O,N and S. (e) TEM images of KTR-CQDs at 20 nm, (f) corresponding HRTEM image at 5 nm displaying the 0.26-nm lattice spacing (SAED patterns in inset indicates the presence of (002), (100), (001), and (004) planes of graphitized carbon), (g) Particle size distribution indicating 3- to 7-nm sizes of KTR-CQDs, (h) EDS assessment represents the existence of C and O.

(a) XRD patterns of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs signifying the (002) graphitic planes of carbon; (b) Raman spectra for PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs representing the D-band (sp3 hybridized) and G band (sp2 hybridized).

(a) FTIR spectra for fabricated CQDs signifying the existence of various functional entities; Deconvoluted spectra of PC-CQDs for (b) C1 s,(c) O1 s,(d) N1 s, and (e) S2p. XPS deconvoluted spectra of KTR-CQDs for (f) C1 s (g) O1 s, and (h) N1 s.
Solid state 13C NMR (a) PC-CQDs and (b) KTR-CQDs showing the existence of sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms with its carbonyl and carboxyl entities.

(a) UV–visible spectra of CQDs; (b) solubility nature of CQDs in water under natural light and under UV light (365 nm); (c) FL spectra of PC-CQDs displaying highest emission at 440 nm and (d) FL spectra of KTR-CQDs displaying highest emission at 450 nm; Time-resolved single-photon counting spectroscopy of (e) PC-CQDs and (f) KTR-CQDs at neutral pH.
Plausible formation mechanism of PC-CQDs (NS@CQDs) and KTR-CQDs (N @ CQDs) from petroleum coke and kitchen tea residue by oxidative ultrasonication, respectively.

(a) Cytotoxicity investigation of both the CQDs showed nearly similar results with highest cell viability of 93%. (b) ROS investigation shows that CQDs had no apparent effect on the production of intracellular ROS levels.
DNA fragmentation study at a concentration of 200 g/mL for 24 h didn't result in any disruption by both the CQDs.
Percentage of radical scavenging activity of KTR-CQDs and PC-CQDs was assessed by deriving from the absorbance of negative control versus test group measured at 517 nm.

Antibacterial influence of KTR-CQDs against bacterial strains (a) Image of Bacillus pseudomycoides bacteria, (b) Image of Glutamibacternicotianae, (c) Image of Phytobacterursingii bacteria, (d) Image of Bacillus wiedmannii bacteria, (e) Image of Rhodococcus soli bacteria; demonstrates that KTR-CQDs have an impact on proliferation of the strains of bacteria, (f) Zone of inhibition of KTR-CQDs towards a variety of bacterial strains exhibit a maximal resistance diameter of 40.33 mm for Rhodococcus soli.
Image showing the Stevia rebaudiana plant growth behavior after various treatments to investigate the effect of CQDs nanofertilizer on plant metabolism.
Effect of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs on (a) Biomass yield, (b) Plant length, (c) Length of leaves of Stevia rebaudiana.
Effect of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs on (a) Chlorophyll and (b) Protein; and plant metabolites (c) Stevioside and (d) Rebaudioside A.
Effect of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs on Antioxidants (a) Superoxide dismutase, (b) Catalase, and (c) Proline.

head.ce:title: Waste-derived high-yield biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots for biological and nanofertiliser applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Petroleum coke and kitchen tea residues are solid waste containing significant carbon contents. In this novel work, we have efficiently fabricated high quantum yield fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from petroleum coke (Pet coke/PC) and kitchen tea residues (KTR) by using a simple and environmentally friendly process of chemical oxidation induced by ultrasonic energy. The average particle dimension of the fabricated PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs are 6 nm and 4.5 nm, respectively revealing highly water-soluble possessing excitation-dependent fluorescence with a maximum photoluminescent quantum yield of 55.14 %. The cell-biology studies demonstrated their excellent biocompatibility with anti-cytotoxic, no significant reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, and are non-genotoxic behaviors. Certain free radical scavenging activity was also observed, representing the presence of sensible antioxidant properties in the waste-derived CQDs. In addition, KTR-CQDs possesses excellent antibacterial properties against different bacterial strains. Further, the phytological investigation with Stevia rebaudiana plants demonstrates the promising potential of waste-derived CQDs to serve as a nanofertilizer, thereby enhancing plant growth. This study opens up a new avenue for gainful and sustainable utilization of industrial waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialization and population growth produce tonnes of solid waste each year, imposing a heavy load on the environment. The handling of solid waste is still a major challenge around the world, and the significant component lies in its socio-ecological consequences. Deprived of a proper and effective waste handling and treatment programme, garbage produced by a variety of human actions, including both domestic and commercial, may be hazardous to health and detrimental to the ecosystem [1]. They produce a large amount of microbial flora close to wastes that can spread infectious diseases to people, plants, and animals. Moreover, composting or burning these wastes produce certain gases that have an unpleasant odor and have the potential to release greenhouse gases like carbon dioxide and methane [2]. Waste minimization, reusing, and rehabilitation are significant waste management techniques for reducing landfill waste and preserving the environment. According to the principles of minimal waste creation and wealth from waste, nanomaterial synthesis from industrial waste/by-products is an ideal process for reutilization.

With the growing concern over sustainable resources, the petrochemical sector encounters difficulties and challenges in managing its by-products like petroleum coke. Petroleum coke (or pet coke) is an outcome of the petroleum industry's coking process, and its yield has been rising annually as crude oil becomes denser. The production of pet coke is expanding significantly as the yield of crude oil and refining industries continue to grow. Pet coke is primarily made of aromatic domains and has a graphitic layered structure. As it possesses significant carbon and minimal ash content, representing it to be utilized as an excellent resource for the fabrication of carbon nanostructures. The total production of petroleum coke in India in the fiscal year 2020–2021 was about 12.655 Mt [3]. Till date, several researchers have performed different experiments for the production of high-value products including nanomaterials from petroleum coke via various activation techniques [4–7]. Wang et al. [7] used petroleum coke to fabricate yellow fluorescing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with excellent selective, sensitive properties showing quick response to monitor Cu2+ ions. CQDs have much potential for various biological uses including delivery of drugs and bio-imaging, and there are still many opportunities for advancement in terms of functionalized CQDs made from cheap and copious raw resources.

According to a report published, India holds the second position in the world with a total production of 1.283 Mt for the fiscal year 2020–2021 [8]. Due to huge tea consumption throughout the world, large amounts of tea waste are produced every day. As such, massive quantities of kitchen and food waste are generated because of globalization and poor disposal of waste. Although kitchen-derived tea residue (KTR) has been successfully used as a fertilizer/ nutritional source for plant growth, it can also serve as a supplier of carbon for the fabrication of value-added products. These waste materials consisting of organic substances are rich in carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen, can be carbonized to form carbon nanomaterials, and thus can be selected as starting materials for the synthesis of CQDs [9]. The idea of reusing and recycling garbage to create industrially relevant CQDs has recently earned a lot of popularity. The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) via using tea industry waste as well as its use as effective imaging and drug carrier for specifically focused cancer therapeutics was reported elsewhere [9]. Zhu et al. [10] also prepared CQDs from discarded tea leaves via the single-step hydrothermal technique. Thus, tea waste, a form of environmental trash, ought to be transformed into carbon nanostructures, which serves as effective sensors along with their biological applications. However, kitchen tea residues from domestic as well as commercial stakeholders have not been seriously attempted for their transformation into carbon nanomaterials as per our literature survey.

Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials including nanotubes, fullerenes, quantum dots, graphene have attracted much attention due to their applications in diversified areas involving bioimaging, sensing, and delivery of drugs. In addition, due to their wide range of applications and simpler synthesis process compared to the other family members, CQDs captured the immersion of the global research network due to outstanding optical qualities, nontoxicity, significant stability, water solubility, associated safety to living tissues, and simple fabrication techniques, leading to a promising future in the realm of fluorescent probes, photocatalysts, metal ions measurement, bioimaging, and delivery of drugs [11–13]. All the above-mentioned properties of CQDs make them superior to the environmentally hazardous conventional semiconductor quantum dots and traditional organic dyes. Carbon dots were accidently identified via the electrophoretic separation of single-wall carbon nanotubes [14]. They typically have quasi-spherical morphology with diameter not more 10 nm and is formed of numerous hydrogenous and oxygenous fragments having sp2/ sp3 hybridized carbon atoms with amorphous to monocrystalline centers [15]. To synthesize CQDs, many synthetic techniques, including arc discharge, laser ablation, microwave, hydrothermal, and chemical oxidation have been investigated [11,13,16,17]. The synthesis of CQDs is mostly preceded by the use of fine carbon constituents or conventional compounds like amino acids, citric acid, carbohydrates, etc. [18]. In a study, the ruthenium-containing carbon dots (Ru-CDs) are reported to have diagnostic and therapeutic functions exhibiting excellent water solubility, intense red emission, and efficient generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [19]. On the otherhand, P-doped, N-doped, and P/N co-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared via hydrothermal method were found to be a promising candidate for cell imaging applications [20]. Quantum dots synthesised utilizing the loblolly pine processing waste, coconut petiole residues etc. had also been used in temperature sensing and Fe3+ detection [21,22].

However, natural carbon resources or industrial by-products seemed to be feasible and inexpensive substitutes to chemical sources to synthesize CQDs on large scale in contrast to the expensive and valuable raw materials used in the conventional methods. Interestingly, conventional techniques for developing CQDs, such as laser exfoliation of graphite, electrochemical conversion of graphite, microwave-induced pyrolysis of saccharide fluid under the influence of polyethylene glycol, hydrothermal treatment of orange juice, glucose, and banana juice, might require difficult fabricated circumstances, a prolonged utilization period, high-priced initial precursors, inadequate yields, and a high risk of failure [23–28]. These methodologies for fabrication also incorporate post-processing procedures using surface passivating chemicals or doping agents to modify the CDs and additional advancements to significantly increase their existing fluorescence efficiency and photostability. In order to avoid the need for high-energy technologies, subsequent post-treatment fabrication, and high-priced precursors, certain chemical procedures are being used progressively [23–28]. Thus, a quick and easy approach that makes "green carbon quantum dots" using natural or natural source derived carbon precursors will allow us to use these nanomaterials effectively while protecting the environment. Thus, CQDs may be produced on large-scales and at cheaper prices, which is particularly beneficial for biomedical or energy-related industrial applications [29]. From the published reports on the environmentally friendly method of making fluorescent CQDs from saccharides, glucose, and coffee grounds have limitations in wide size distribution, high-priced preparation, small-scale production, and poor chemical durability [26,27,30]. As natural sources such as plant-based or fossil-based sources contain a large number of heteroatoms, utilising these sources to make carbon nanomaterials has become popular in recent years.

Although chemical oxidation is one of the suitable methods that is efficient, feasible, and simple to scale up, but is not so active in terms of breaking down the aromatic carbon framework, which is particularly true for accurate regulation of the shape and size of CQDs with higher luminescence [31]. In this research article, we describe the fabrication of biocompatible CQDs from petroleum coke (PC-CQDs) and kitchen tea residue (KTR-CQDs) via an oxidation chemical process enhanced by ultrasonic energy. Petroleum coke, which contains stacked layers of graphitic materials and kitchen tea residue, composed of mixtures of several organic molecules has been effectively transformed into well-dispersed CQDs under the influence of oxidizing agent H2O2 and ultrasonic energy, resulting in water-soluble, adjustable fluorescent CQDs with uniform size and high quantum yield (QY). Importantly, we also outline the fabrication of "green nanoparticles" for waste disposal. As per our best of literature survey, there have been no report that focuses on the biological assessment of the CQDs derived from petroleum coke and kitchen tea residue. This study also includes the in-vitro cytotoxicity, genotoxicity, reactive oxygen species, and free radical scavenging activity of the synthesized CQDs along with the impact of the CQDs on phytotoxicity, plant metabolites, and antioxidant properties.

Waste materials including petroleum coke and kitchen tea residue samples were chosen for this study. The petroleum coke sample (referred to as PC) was collected from Guwahati Refinery, India. Kitchen tea residue samples (referred to as KTR) were collected from households and hotels in local areas within Golaghat district (India). The PC samples were first crushed to < 0.425-mm sizes by adopting the available standard method. The KTR samples were first carbonised at 300°C for 90 min in a muffle furnace and then ground to < 0.425-mm size for further analysis.

Waste material based CQDs were prepared using an ultrasonic-induced chemical oxidation approach by mixing 100 g of each precursor, i.e., PC (moisture content of 0.96 wt%) and KTR (moisture content of 9.28 wt%) with 1000 mL of H2O2 (concentration 30 %) maintaining ice-cold condition. The chemically treated products were ultrasonicated, followed by neutralizing the mixture with NH3 solution. The resultant solutions were vacuum filtered using Whatman 41, and finally passed through a 1 kDa membrane in an ultrafiltration system. The ultrafiltered samples were then concentrated by using a rotary evaporator to isolate the produced carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The fabricated petroleum coke-derived CQDs and kitchen tea residue-derived CQDs were referred to as PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs in the subsequent analysis. CQDs synthesized from PC and KTR were observed to be pale yellow in color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterizations and quantum yield determination
ce:section.ce:para: The examination of the waste materials as well as the derived CQDs was carried out by following the advanced level analytical characterization techniques reported in our prior studies [32–34]. In order to determine the quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CQDs, the standard procedure was adopted by using quinine sulphate and sulphuric acids [32–34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay, DNA fragmentation and intracellular ROS study
ce:section.ce:para: Here, for the cellular assay, rodent hepatocyte cell line, CC1 (Sigma-Aldrich) was used and prepared following the methodology reported elsewhere [34]. AlamarBlue™ reduction bioassay was used to analyse the cell viability test [34]. The cell viability, DNA fragmentation, and ROS levels studies of the fabricated CQDs were conducted as per the procedures mentioned elsewhere [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPPH free radical scavenging assay of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The test samples (KTR-CQD, and PC-CQD) were evaluated for their scavenging activity on stable organic free radical DPPH (2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazil) [35]. 100 µl of DPPH (125 µM) solution was mixed with 100 µl of the test sample with a set of different concentrations (10 µg/mL to 500 µg/mL) in 96 well plates, followed by incubation for 1 h in dark condition at 37 °C. After that, the absorbance was assessed at 517 nm in microplate reader.
All test’s data were obtained from triplicate wells of each group carried out in three independent experiments.

Each test was performed in triplicates and ascorbic acid was considered as the control. The radical scavenging activity (% RSA) was evaluated as below:
% RSA = [Absorbance (Blank) - Absorbance (Test)]/ [Absorbance (Blank)] x 100
Percentage of % RSA was calculated against the concentration and the IC50 value (µg/mL) was estimated from the graph.

The antibacterial efficacy was evaluated for the fabricated CQDs by treating against varied bacterial strains, which includes gram positive strains such as Bacillus pseudomycoides (N43), Glutamibacternicotianae (N47), Bacillus wiedmannii (N63), Rhodococcus soli (N112) and gram-negative strain Phytobacterursingii (N62). This study had been performed using the agar well diffusion method [36–38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on plant growth

ce:section.ce:para: The experimental design was prepared adopting an established procedures [34]. In a greenhouse, Stevia rebaudiana plants were cultivated for 60 days on sandy loam soil (Temperature: 27/22 ± 2 °C; day/night, humidity: 65–80 %, light: 16 h). 30 mL of two different concentrations of CQDs (75 mg/L and 150 mg/L) were sprayed over the stevia plants. After 90 days of cultivation, fresh leaves were chosen from the plants to examine their enzymatic activities. At the beginning of flowering, the plants were harvested, cleaned, and weight and length were measured. The plant tissues (T1, T2, T3, and T4) were dried and grounded. Here, T1 (75 mg/L) and T2 (150 mg/L) represents the use of PC-CQDs, while T3 (75 mg/L) and T4 (150 mg/L) represents the use of KTR-CQDs.

The experiments were assessed following the similar procedures as mentioned in our previous studies to estimate the plant parameters [34]. The method outlined by Arnon and Whatley [39] was used to measure the amount of total chlorophyll. Antioxidant enzymes such as superoxide dismutase and catalase, which are activated by stress, were studied using the methods described elsewhere [40,41]. In addition, proline and protein content were accomplished using the techniques outlined by Lowry et al. [42] and Bates et al. [43], respectively. The experimental scheming to investigate metabolites (Stevioside and Rebaudioside A.) in plants were performed by following the previously mentioned procedures [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physico-chemical characteristics of the waste materials
ce:section.ce:para: The physico-chemical characterizations including the spectroscopic and electron microscopic evaluations are presented in the supplementary information (SI). Table S-1 (see supplementary information) shows that the sample PC and KTR mainly comprised of carbon and hydrogen. The carbon content of PC and KTR are 87.30 wt% and 67.82 wt%, respectively. The presence of functional groups including CC, C-H, C-S, O-H, N-H, and CH2/ CH3 in the framework of the raw waste materials was confirmed from FTIR spectroscopy (see Fig. S1 in supplementary information). XRD patterns display a sharp peak around 26o that is attributed to the (002) graphitic lattice planes indicating the crystalline carbon (see Fig. S2 in supplementary information). Moreover, KTR also shows a sharp peak at 43o attributed to the (100) planes of disordered carbon. The Raman spectra of PC (Fig. S3a in supplementary information) confirms the presence of D-bands (∼1367 cm-1) and G-bands (∼1600 cm-1); and KTR also displays (see Fig. S3b in supplementary information) the occurrence of D-bands (∼1370 cm-1) and G-bands (∼1595 cm-1), where D-band represents the existence of structural imperfections, while G-band indicates the sp2 carbon frameworks, representing the properties of graphitic constituents in the feedstock. XPS scan spectra (Fig. S4a, b in supplementary information) for both PC and KTR samples confirm the existence of carbon (284 eV), oxygen (532 eV) and nitrogen (402 eV), while PC also shows an additional minor peak for sulphur at 164 eV. The de-convoluted spectra of C1 s (Fig. S4c,d in supplementary information) depicts the presence of sp2 C-C at about 283.7 eV for both the source materials, while KTR also displays a peak at about 292 eV representing the O-CO functional group. The deconvoluted O1 s (Fig. S4e, f in supplementary information) peak signifies the organic C-O linkage with a binding energy of 531.3 eV. This peak denotes the existence of aromatic complexes connected by oxygen-based linking bonds. Solid-state 13C NMR of PC and KTR samples (see Fig. S6a, b in supplementary information) shows frequencies in between 5 and 45 ppm and 4–30 ppm, respectively, which are attributed to the aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms. Both the samples show signals arising at about 85–160 ppm contributing mainly to the sp2 carbon atoms. Moreover, PC additionally shows signals at 210–240 ppm which is attributed to the carbonyl group present on the feed material. The SEM images of petroleum by-products (see Fig. S7a in supplementary information) displays the existence of irregularly oriented micron-sized units with uneven mineral agglomerations that resemble amorphous fragments of carbon. The SEM image of biomass waste (see Fig. S7c in supplementary information) represents minute pores that may arise during the carbonization process. Moreover, the EDS study of PC depicts the abundant of carbon atoms along with oxygen and sulphur (see Fig. S7b in supplementary information), while the KTR confirms the existence of a high amount of carbon and oxygen along with trace amounts of potassium, magnesium, manganese, calcium, and aluminium (see Fig. S7d in supplementary information).

The waste sources/ by-products, i.e., kitchen tea residue (KTR) and petroleum coke (PC) serve as a sustainable and inexpensive feedstock for synthesising fluorescent CQDs. As stated, the PC and KTR samples were first oxidized using H2O2 and were further neutralized using NH3 solution under constant stirring. The fabricated PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs are functionalised with hydrophilic groups, which are pale yellow in color when diluted under natural light. It exhibits prominent solubility and stability in water without any precipitation even after 6 months.

The micro-as well as nano-structural features of the fabricated CQDs were analysed by performing the TEM analysis for examination of their formation. Fig. 1a shows the quasi-spherically shaped uniform and mono-dispersed dots for PC-CQDs with crystal lattices of 0.263 nm (see Fig. 1b) indicating their crystallinity, and SAED patterns (inset Fig. 1b) shows the presence of 0.331-, 0.263-, 0.221-, and 0.162-nm lattice spacing that resembles the (002), (100), (001), and (004) graphitic carbon planes, respectively. The particle dimensions of PC-CQDs are ranged within 3 and 10 nm, having an average dimension of 6 nm, satisfying the Gaussian distribution (Fig. 1c). The EDS elemental mapping (see Fig. S7a-d in supplementary information) of PC-CQDs displays the existence of C, O, N, and S, similar to the elements confirmed via the EDS analysis (Fig. 1d). In case of KTR-CQDs, particles were observed to have quasi-spherically shaped with uniform distribution as displayed in Fig. 1e. The insets in Fig. 1f depict certain crystallinity with lattice fridges of 0.263 nm, and SAED patterns (inset Fig. 1f) depict the presence of 0.331-, 0.265-, 0.227-, and 0.164-nm lattice spacings that correspond to (002), (100), (001) and (004) graphitic planes of carbon. The particle dimensions of KTR-CQDs are within 0 and 7 nm, having an average dimension of 4.5 nm, satisfying the Gaussian distribution (Fig. 1g). EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S8e-g) depicts the existence of C, O, and N in the fabricated CQDs that is also confirmed by the EDS analysis (Fig. 1h). It is to be mentioned that the particle size of PC-CQDs were found to be larger than that of KTR-CQDs that may be due to the use of different precursor materials. The difference in size might be attributed to the composition and structure of the precursors. Petroleum coke is primarily made of aromatic domains and possesses graphitic layered structure, these graphitized layers cannot be easily exfoliated thereby leading to particles of slightly larger size. While kitchen tea residue might have a more heterogeneous composition with organic matter and other components, resulting in smaller and more irregularly shaped CQDs during synthesis. Although the methods used to synthesize CQDs also play a crucial role, here almost similar synthesis technique has been considered for their validation in getting the CQDs. The structural features of the fabricated CQDs were found to be analogous to those previously described for CQD production [7,44].

The XRD patterns shown in Fig. 2a portray a wide peak centered with 2 theta value of 23.87º and 24.50º for PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs with layer spacing of 0.372 and 0.363 nm, respectively, indicating its equivalence to (002) layer spacing. The peak observed signifies the conjugated sp2 carbon skeleton, representing the aromatic compounds. Identification of oxygen-rich functional groups correlates to amorphous carbon phases that justify the variation from the original graphitic peak (2θ = 26.4º) [28,32]. This accordance with the SAED patterns displayed in Fig. 1b (inset) and 1 f (inset).

Raman spectroscopic analysis is a crucial method for determining structural imperfections in a carbon assembly. Here, the spectra primarily show three major absorption bands (Fig. 2b) for the fabricated CQDs. From the spectra, representative bands arising at 1428 cm-1 (D-band), and 1632 cm-1 (G-band) for PC-CQDs; and 1425 cm-1 (D-band), and 1615 cm-1 (G-band) for KTR-CQDs were observed. The D-band originated from disordered surfaces/ lattice comprising carbon in its sp3 hybridization, and the G-band is associated with the vibrations arising from the sp2 carbon structure of the CQDs framework. Moreover, a broad band was also observed within 1275–1360 cm-1 in the CQDs samples due to the disordered sp3 carbon framework agrees with the result reported by Korepanov et al. [45]. It can be mentioned that oxidation and the existence of surface hetero-entities cause defects in sp2 carbon frameworks of CQDs.

Using the FTIR analysis ( Fig. 3a), it is possible to identify the functional groups present on the surfaces of fabricated CQDs. The wide absorption spectrum identified at 3338 and 3028 cm-1 for PC-CQDs and 3187 and 3010 cm-1 for KTR-CQDs, are due to the absorption peaks of O-H and N-H bonds [46]. Both the fabricated CQDs depict a strong band at around 2855 cm-1, as a result of C-H vibrational modes, while found to be more pronounced in PC-CQDs. A strong peak at 1642 cm-1 is attributed to the CC, while band at 1566 cm-1 results from asymmetric N-H deformation. Moreover, bands at 1410 cm-1 and 1062 cm-1 results from bending vibrations of C-N and stretching vibrations of C-O [47]. Additionally, PC-CQDs shows another peak at 615 cm-1 that ascribes to C-S bond [34]. All the evidence obtained from the FT-IR analysis indicates the existence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino functional entities at the surfaces of CQDs indicating good hydrophilicity.

In order to evaluate the surface functionalization, chemical bonding, and elemental constituents of both the derived CQDs, the XPS study were performed. The XPS survey scan (Fig. S9 in supplementary information) depict mainly the presence of C, O, N, and S with pronounced peaks for C1 s and O1 s at 284 and 400 eV, while KTR-CQDs show the weaker signal for N2p at 531 eV, and PC-CQDs shows weaker peaks for N2p and S2p at 531 and 169 eV. High-resolution C1s peak for PC-CQDs (Fig. 3b) were fitted into four different peaks to analyse the chemical states of C at 283.7 eV (CC), 286.1 eV (C-O/C-N/C-S), 288.1 eV (O-CO), and 292.1 eV (O-CO) [5]. The other peak at 286.1 eV represents the C-O/C-N/C-S bonds. Fig. 3f shows that the high-resolution C1s spectra for KTR-CQDs identified pronounced peaks at 283.9 eV, 287 eV, and 291.7 eV ascribing for C = C, C = N/C = O, and O-C = O bonds, respectively [5]. Moreover, O1s core-level spectrum of PC-CQDs (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into 521.4 eV (organic C-O), while KTR-CQDs (Fig. 3g) deconvoluted to 530.7 eV (organic C-O) and 535 eV (C-O-H) respectively. Both PC-CQDs (Fig. 3d) and KTR-CQDs (Fig. 3h) showed high resolution N1 s spectrum with peaks nearby 399 and 401 eV assigned for C-N and pyrrolic N entities [48,49]. In addition, deconvoluted S2p spectra (Fig. 3e) for PC-CQDs signifies two distinct peaks for SO and C-S at 167.1 and 168.3 eV [34]. Thus, efficient self-co-doping of N and S on PC-CQDs and N on KTR-CQDs were revealed from the results attained via FTIR and XPS analyses. Therefore, these elements (N and S) can be denoted to as self-doped in the structural frameworks of fabricated CQDs that proved to be obtained from the elements existing in petroleum coke and kitchen-derived tea residue which had also been established by the basic elemental study, electron beam, and spectroscopic analysis (see Table S-1, Figs. S-1, S-4a, S-4b, S-7b and S-7d). From XPS and FTIR analyses, these carbon nanostructures have been confirmed to possess sp2 hybridized frameworks with structurally modified oxygeneous fragments for instance carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl moieties [7,50].

Solid-state 13C NMR of CQDs show the existence of distinct chemical environments as depicted in Fig. 4a and b. Using 13C NMR spectroscopy, it is possible to distinguish between sp3 and sp2 carbons and thereby, be able to attain structural knowledge about carbon nanomaterials. From the spectra of both the samples (PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs), signals ranging from 5 to 80 ppm corresponds to sp3 carbon atoms. Also, multiple peaks appear in the range 100–140 ppm owing to sp2 carbon atoms, while arising between 120 and 140 ppm originate primarily from aromatic (polycyclic) carbons atoms. Moreover, peaks within 160–185 ppm suggest the existence of carbonyl/ carboxyl/ amide groups [51]. Hereby, it can be mentioned that PC-CQDs have a higher intensity of sp2 carbon atom signal about that of KTR-CQDs, while the KTR-CQDs are highly functionalized as it has a higher intensity of CO group signal compared to that of PC-CQDs. From the 13C NMR spectra of raw Petroleum coke (PC) and Kitchen tea residue (KTR), we can predict that their structures constitute of sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms along with polycyclic aromatic frameworks. Moreover, raw source PC is also built up of CO functional groups. As such, it can be assumed that the nanostructures are formed by the ultrasonic chemical oxidation of these raw carbon precursors, thereby enhancing the improvement in the chemical environment of the fabricated carbon nanostructures. Subsequently, confirming the oxidative generation of marginal functional groups on the fabricated CQDs having sp2 and sp3 carbons centers.

Thus, it can be mentioned here that the EDS, elemental mapping, FTIR, and XPS studies of PC-CQDs contains C, O, N, and S; while KTR-CQDs contains C, O, and N. This chemistry could be referred as formation of self-co-doped CQDs as these elements are derived from the structural composition/ framework of petroleum coke and tea residues itself.

The photophysical features of the fabricated CQDs were examined via their pronounced absorption and emission characteristics, as shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5a represents absorption bands nearby 230 and 285 nm for both the fabricated CQDs. The spectra for PC-CQDs shows an absorption band from 220 to 230 nm and for KTR-CQDs from 250 to 280 nm which is ascribed to π-π * shift of aromatic sp2 carbon domains. Moreover, both the synthesised CQDs show strong absorption at 280–320 nm, which is ascribed to n-π * (nO 2p–π * and nN 2p–π * ) shift of CO and CN [32,52]. This indicates CQDs resulting in assorted sizes with various functional domains that are well suited to the results of TEM and FTIR/ XPS investigation. Under natural light (Fig. 5b), the aqueous suspensions of the fabricated waste-derived CQDs, i.e., PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs are pale yellow in color, while irradiating under a UV source of 365 nm, the CQDs display blue-green (or cyan) emission.

Fig. 5c and d depicts the fluorescence emission of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs, indicating the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with respect to studies reported earlier [34,53,54]. When the excitation wavelength increases from 260 to 360 nm in PC-CQDs and from 280 to 380 nm in KTR-CQDs, the FL emission wavelength slightly increases, corresponding to the red-shift. The spectra show maximum intensity within 440–450 nm signifying the blue-green (cyan) color. The fluorescence (FL) intensity and wavelength of maximum emission are largely dependent on the wavelength of excitation, emerging from the surface states of various functional units containing oxygen, for instance CO, -OH, C-O, and O-C-O, that resulted in varied surface state energy levels amongst π and π * of CC domains. This demonstrates that the waste-derived CQDs have a wide range of nano-sized particles and defect states on these nanoparticles. Variable particle dimensions or chemical entities on the surfaces of CQDs can be used to assess the FL characteristics of CQDs. The presence of fluorophore units in aromatic assemblies has been confirmed by FTIR and XPS analyses. The maximum FL emission is observed to show blue fluorescence due to π–π * , nN2p–σ * , and nO2p–π * transition linked with sp2 entities of aromatic framework, amino groups, and protonated carboxyl groups [55,56]. Furthermore, the nO2p–π * transition resulting from de-protonated carboxyl units is expected to display cyan fluorescence. The existence of many fluorescent centers in these CQDs, increased the possibility of excitation-dependent FL emission, and also plausible to illustrate the various emissions from distinct fluorescent centers.

Time-resolved single-photon counting spectroscopy is used for estimation of average FL lifetimes of as-synthesized CQDs. Fig. 5e and f represent the FL decay curves with lifetimes of 2.62 and 3.10 ns for PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs, respectively. The variation in FL lifetime highlighted the effect of varied fluorophores or energy levels present in the carbon nanostructures, as it is predicted to have equivalent carbon centers in both the fabricated CQDs [32]. The measured lifetimes recommended the use of these CQDs in biological activities. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was evaluated by using quinine sulfate as a standard (QY= 54 %) and sulfuric acid solution following the procedure reported elsewhere [32–34]. The FL quantum yield (QY) for PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs were measured to be 55.14 % and 8.95 %. Based on these findings, we may conclude that CQDs obtained from petroleum coke and kitchen tea residue via this fabrication technique have a greater QY than those synthesised by other synthesis methods [5,7]. All the above observed exceptional optical properties and their high QY are closely linked to the excellent-quality traits associated with CQDs, for instance outstanding electron-donating modification at edges compared to the base sites. The concentrations of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs were found to be 7.11 mg/mL and 8.11 mg/mL, respectively. After comparison of excitation and emission spectrum of PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs, it is observed that the optimum fluorescence intensity is observed at 445 nm when excited at 320 nm (for PC-CQDs) and at 442 nm (for KTR-CQDs) when excited at 340 nm (see Fig. S5).

Magnitude of electrostatic repulsion of fabricated CQDs amongst neighboring charged particles in the distributed system was measured using zeta-potential experiments. The zeta potentials of the synthesized CQDs (see Fig. S10 in supplementary information) were found to be − 13 and − 28.2 eV, signifying the aqueous suspension of CQDs to be highly stable. Negative values were obtained as a result of the abundance of surface functional groups with oxygen entities connected to these CQDs. Their solubility and stability in aqueous conditions are significantly improved by the hydrophilic groups, enabling them to be much simpler for use for biological and medicinal processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CQDs formation from the waste
ce:section.ce:para: To effectively utilise petroleum coke and kitchen tea residue, the understanding of their structural aspects is essential even though the chemical structures are difficult to properly characterize. Petroleum coke is mainly composed of tiny graphitic crystals comprising polycyclic aromatic clusters along with aliphatic fraction. While the kitchen-derived tea residues also possess aromatic clusters that are rich in carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen, their microstructures comprise interconnections of organic carbon that bind the crystalline fragments together. The plausible mechanism as depicted in the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 6 illustrates the chemical oxidation with hydrogen peroxide generating OH . radicals followed by treatment with ultrasonic energy that helps in cleaving both the graphitic and non-graphitic hydrocarbons leading to polymorphic reactions resulting in partially crystalline nanosized carbon structures, i.e., CQDs, and also releases CO2 and H2O in the reaction process.

The aromatic fragmentation can break down into C2 units, leading to polymorphic interactions that subsequently generate CQDs. By allowing the oxidant (H2O2) to permeate the samples, this method oxidized the interlayers of the graphitic framework of petroleum coke and the organic components of kitchen tea residue. As the oxidant permeated deeper into the material, the interlayer gap and defoliation degree widened, breaking bridge bonds like -O, -S, -CH2-O, and -CH2-CH2-, etc., resulting in CQDs. These results were also linked to ultrasonic waves, which cause ultrasonic energy to pass through the reaction slurry. As a result, bubble cavitation arose, which is dependable for causing the bonds in precursors to break down. These nanostructures were thought to be formed because of processes that disrupted weaker covalent interaction, hydrogen bonds, and loosened the π-π bindings. Moreover, phenomenon of thermodynamically stable phase nucleation at the nanoscale level has been theorized for the development of CQDs [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicological properties of fabricated CQDs from waste
ce:section.ce:para: The level of toxicity caused by these fabricated nanoparticles while associating with cells is amongst the most essential characteristics to assess the usability of the waste-derived CQDs. The cytotoxic potential was investigated and resulted in high cell viability (> 90 %) for rodent hepatocyte cell lines, when incubated for 24 h with different dosages of CQDs ( Fig. 7a). Comparative analysis with that of the control, signifies the non-toxic nature of the CQDs for each concentration. PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs showed nearly alike outcomes having maximum cell feasibility of 93.36 (20 µg/mL) and 92.74 % (100 µg/mL), respectively. No significant rise in cytotoxicity has been observed at higher concentrations, thereby indicating good cell viability. The results of dose-dependent in vitro cell viability study of fabricated CQDs in the tested cell lines for 24 h are in agreement with the previously reported works [34,58]. CQDs are favorable for clinical uses owing to the existence of NH2 units [58]. The absence of cytotoxicity may be attributed for utilization of harmless sources and the successful elimination of undesirable elemental contaminants. It suggests that these CQDs have similar bio-existence and are benign to cell lines, indicating that they could be used for bioimaging applications.

When varied amounts of these CQDs were used to study the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), there was no notable variation in the formation of intracellular ROS levels when related to the control study (Fig. 7b). This indicates that the CQDs did not enhance the production of ROS, which is essential in the oxidative stress pathway. This toxicology assessment also implies that these CQDs are suitable biological activities.

The profile of DNA fragmentation was analysed to explore the genotoxicity of synthesized CQDs. Fig. 8 shows no indication of DNA defect in CQDs-induced cells if compared to conventional cells after 24 h of incubation with a concentration of 200 g/mL of each CQD.

Radical scavenging activity is thought to be important for the prevention of cellular oxidative stress. Carbon nanoparticles bearing prominent semiconductor property acts as a potential candidate for altering the ROS by receiving or transferring an electron [59]. Nanoparticles possessing antioxidant properties are observed to alter the generation of free radicals. DPPH radical scavenging assay is the standard method to estimate the ROS scavenging efficacy of any test sample. The result clearly shows that KTR-CQDs have higher DPPH radical scavenging activity than PC-CQDs ( Fig. 9). Among both samples, KTR-CQDs exhibited the highest DPPH radical scavenging activity. Estimated IC50 value of KTR-CQDs is found to be 395.31 µg/mL. The findings of this investigation demonstrated all CQDs have some degree of free radical scavenging property, and this may be useful in preventing possible cellular damage inflicted by cellular free radicals. The deprotonation from the functional entities such as -OH and -COOH, present on the exterior of CQDs to stabilize DPPH molecules may be the reason for the mechanism. As a result of this phenomenon, free radicals are generated on the exterior of CQDs. These radicals in turn may be stabilised by the rearranging of functional entities or by the resonance of unpaired electrons [59]. Further, cell-based detailed mechanistic studies and animal investigations are essential for improved evaluation of their potential applications as antioxidant agents.

The inhibition zone analysis was used to investigate the antibacterial properties of CQDs against Gram-positive (N43, N47, N63, N112) bacteria and Gram-negative (N62). The addition of 20-µl KTR-CQDs shows high antibacterial activity by inhibiting the growth of all the bacteria strains, as indicated in Fig. 10a-e. The diameters of the zone of inhibition of KTR-CQDs towards specific types of strains are shown in Fig. 10f, with the strain Rhodococcus soli (N112) representing the highest zone of inhibition with a diameter of 40.33 mm compared to others. It is observed that Gram-positive bacteria are comparatively more susceptible to KTR-CQDs than Gram-negative bacteria, and the results are consistent with formerly published findings [60]. The inhibition shown by KTR-CQDs is somewhat superior or comparable to that of other nanoparticles that have been studied [38,61]. While Fig. S10 depicts that PC-CQDs (20 mL) couldn’t restrain bacterial growth against any strain, implying the non-existence of antibacterial characteristics on its own.

Here, it is observed that KTR-CQDs show better anti-bacterial behavior compared to that of PC-CQDs. This may be possibly due to the existence of polyphenols/anti-microbial components, in particular flavonoids present in the chemical-structural composition of tea, which may avail its presence in the kitchen tea residue-derived CQDs that are responsible for their inhibitory effect against pathogenic bacteria [60–62]. Moreover, Gram-negative bacteria are more resistant to CQDs, which might be due to changes in the cell wall structure. Gram-negative strains possess exterior phospholipidic membrane that works like an obstacle towards several external compounds, including drugs, but Gram-positive bacteria have an external layer of peptidoglycan that doesn’t possess adequate penetrability barrier and consequently are more accessible. Moreover, the synthesized CQDs show antimicrobial features because of the existence of functional entities such as carboxyl, and amine connected to CQDs can hinder bacterial growth as was found to be reported earlier by Tang et al. [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of CQDs as nanofertiliser on plant growth promotion was performed on the Stevia rebaudiana plants at two different concentrations. Fig. 11 presents images of Stevia rebaudiana plant under investigation to survey the influence of PC-CQDS and KTR-CQDs on plant metabolism. Primary investigations to ascertain the influence of CQDs begin with assessing the physiological development measure such as biomass yield, root weight, shoot weight and length. Fig. 12a depicts the enhancement of biomass production with the addition of CQDs when compared with the controlled assessment. The influence of different hydrophilic groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino functional entities on CQDs surface is considered as being responsible for plant's growth (Fig. 12b) and substantial improvement in the length of the leaves (Fig. 12c) when related to that of the control study. These groups provide an extra binding affinity for the nutrients and water molecules via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attractions to assess the plant and controlled release. Therefore, adequate plant hydration can promote accelerated plant growth. According to the latest studies, cabbage seeds when exposed to nano-diamonds enhanced with boron before sowing have a good impact on germination and its development [64]. Additionally, it is reported that carbon dots, even when enriched with As, can help the plant growth to about 66 % [65]. Moreover, the photosynthetic activity in wheat plants was not affected by the carbon nanomaterials like detonated nano-diamonds as was reported by previous toxicological study [66].

Moreover, these CQDs can potentially enhance the chlorophyll concentration and protein content of stevia plants as presented in Fig. 13a and b. In instance of metabolites, compared to Stevioside (Fig. 13c), the treated plants contained more Rebaudioside A (Fig. 13d). It is to be mentioned that the ratio of Rebaudioside/Stevioside was 0.048 in our control study. The fabricated PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs altered the ratio from 0.033 to 0.095. It indicates that PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs may alter the synthesis pathway of two metabolites. Moreover, the action of main antioxidants such as Superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase, and proline was assessed. In Fig. 14, it is observed that under the CQDs treatment, there was a decrease in the antioxidant property when related to the reference study as the concentration of SOD, catalase, and proline were found to increase, thereby providing stress to plants. Proteins are also broken into amino acids which are transformed to proline for storing under stress. This indicates that PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs could be used for the alteration of metabolite pathway synthesis genes. The CQDs may also be utilized as nutrients and microbes carrier for plant development promoters.

It is important to note that identification of CQDs uptake by plants is possible through the consistent and distinctive fluorescence signal of CQDs. Additionally, CQDs can be detected in plants and utilized as UV-to-blue light modification media in plants to expedite the photosynthesis process owing to their fluorescence property [67]. Thus, the bio-imaging of plant cells can also be done with the help of CQDs. Based on the aforementioned findings, these CQDs possess the potential to serve as nanofertilizers as well as transporters of nutrients and microorganisms for enhancing plant growth and yield.

In conclusion, we have developed a low-cost and eco-friendly ultrasonic-assisted chemical oxidation method to fabricate CQDs (PC-CQDs and KTR-CQDs) by utilizing kitchen tea residue and industrial petroleum coke. The CQDs displayed good water solubility because of the oxygen-rich surface functionalities demonstrating excitation wave-dependent blue/bluish-green fluorescence under UV light. The fabricated CQDs exhibit very low cytotoxicity, no significant elevation of the ROS generation, free radical scavenging property and anti-bacterial property. These waste-derived CQDs also possess the potential to serve as nanofertilizers for enhancing plant growth rate. Our present study encourages the growth of sustainable applications along with environmental protection by highlighting the significance of the production of valuable carbon nano products by using such waste sources that are typically regarded as of low-value keeping an eye on the waste-to-wealth endeavor.

Monikankana Saikia: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-review, Writing – original draft. Abhishek Hazarika: Formal analysis, Investigation. Kallol Roy: Formal analysis, Investigation. Puja Khare: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Anjum Dihingia: Formal analysis. Rituraj Konwar: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Binoy K Saikia: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
